Ai-powered digital twin in the industrial IoT
=============================================

Highlights
----------

*   •The artificial intelligence underpinning the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). 
*   •Vertical hierarchical computing for digital twins (DT) 
*   •A hot issue is merging DT and IIoT concepts. 
*   •Machine learning models for multivariate time series forecasting. 

Abstract
--------

The rapid emergence of the smart industry hides numerous challenges that need to be addressed promptly. In the transition between two industrial eras (Industry 4.0 and Industry 5.0), hands-on applications of digital twins in intelligent manufacturing are pivotal in enhancing efficiency, optimizing operations, and ensuring sustainability. The paper presents the digital twin (DT) concept in a vertical Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) framework powered by machine learning (ML) models for time series forecasting. According to DT needs and hierarchical data processing, as well as edge, fog, and cloud computing, the paper presents state-of-the-art ML models and algorithms. Real-time and low-latency requirements of smart edge devices and monitoring systems force the selection of DT models powered by ML models for time series processing and forecasting. Stronger computer resources characterize the IIoT fog level. At this level, DT models should be supported by techniques and methods for parameter selection, correlation analysis, and heatmap visualization that facilitates time series processing. Special attention is devoted to developing a novel multivariate-time-series prediction method. This method should enable parameter prediction which cannot be directly measured. The method was validated based on several real-time series.

1. Introduction
---------------

In the mature age of the 4th Industrial Revolution, the introduction of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) required comprehensive changes in the industrial environment regarding networking and logical connection between computers, machines, and experts using advanced control and management strategies, fast data processing, and timely decision-making. Today, in the transitional between the 4th and 5th industrial revolutions, with the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) techniques and IIoT technologies, digital twins (DT) have recently experienced a growth revolution [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0005). Digital twins are virtual replicas of physical systems that can mimic their actions and behaviors and aid in decision-making [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0010). Thanks to the advancements in information and communication technology, complex system optimization problems become solvable with the introduction of hands-on digital twin technology. As a result of the huge data science achievements, including cloud computing, and internet devices, DT is now widely applicable to process monitoring and data processing in both virtual and physical contexts [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0015).

Adapting traditional control and management concepts to the predominantly vertical IIoT concept [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0020) is a scientific, technological, and engineering challenge. The core of DT lies in the bidirectional link of assets (physical space) and models (virtual space). DT has accelerated the fusion of digital technology and traditional industry [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0025). As IoT has entered industrial facilities, DT has emerged as a workable and efficient approach to management, corrective control, fine parameter tuning, and early fault detection. The vertical IIoT framework predominantly comprises edge, fog, and cloud computing levels [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0020). Within the IIoT context, the DT concept has emerged as a pivotal innovation, offering unprecedented opportunities for real-time monitoring, fault prediction, parameter optimization, and power efficiency. The main advantage of the IIoT is the hierarchical processing of data and information, which also creates conditions for the parallel and simultaneous application of many DT models at the edge, fog, and cloud levels. Regarding DT networked systems, the layered architecture contains three interacting components, i.e., the physical layer, the DT layers, and the DT networks layer [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0030).

Digital twins are trying to improve existing management and monitoring systems with the support of the latest smart technologies, artificial intelligence, and real-time exchange of large amounts of data to improve monitoring, process control, and fault prediction. Smart manufacturing and maintenance are a field of research primarily driven by DT concepts that incorporate optimization methods [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0035) and theories such as: Markov decision process [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0040), Bellman principle [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0045), fuzzy decision-making, etc. DT applications for maintenance have significantly increased in the Industry 4.0 era [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0050). Furthermore, industries with manufacturing and greater maintenance costs are more interested in investing in DT technology.

The need for hierarchical architectures of DT-enabled intelligent manufacturing for practically applicable DT concepts has recently been recognized [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0055). The necessity for multi-model fusion in DT technology has also been acknowledged [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0060).

There are open challenges in IIoT, big data management, and analysis in cloud environments [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0065). However, there is a lack of functional mutual connectivity between management and decision-making concepts related to industrial IoT and digital twins. To fill this conceptual gap, this paper presents two innovations. The first pertains to the DT and IIoT integrated concepts for large industrial and power systems. The second contribution involves the selection and practical application of artificial intelligence models and algorithms that are of interest for edge and fog levels, which are lower hierarchical computing levels closer to manufacturing processes, systems, and devices.

As stated in the paper, the paper deals with strategic control, monitoring, management, and decision-making concepts in smart power plants using integrated DT models in the vertical IIoT framework. A comprehensive hierarchical DT concept with hands-on applicable state-of-the-art data science and machine learning techniques, models, and algorithms are presented.

Here, in the beginning, it is necessary to clarify the terminological dilemma, digital twin or digital twins? The authors used the term “digital twin” for a single ML model or a smaller group of ML models, while “digital twins” were used for a bunch of ML models at fog and edge levels.

The effectiveness of digital twins heavily depends on their ability to process and forecast multivariate time series data from various sensors monitoring the electricity production process and equipment. ML models, such as Long Short-Term Memory networks [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0070), Transformer-based architectures [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0075), and Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN), can analyze historical sensor data and predict failures before they occur.

Traditional DTs rely on simplistic predictive models for dynamic processes or equipment. Multivariate time series prediction models address a more complex approach thanks to the following:
*   •Handling High-Dimensional Data 
*   •Capturing Complex Dependencies and Interactions 
*   •Enhancing Predictive Maintenance and Decision-Making 
*   •Making DTs more adaptive and resilient 
*   •Improving DT simulations by more complex system parameters 

Multivariate forecasting techniques can predict electricity demand based on historical load patterns, weather conditions, and grid conditions. ML forecasting models enable real-time process parameter tuning, optimizing operational and maintenance efficiency. The data-driven approach enables synchronization between the dynamic model and the actual physical system, facilitating accurate predictions and efficient energy management [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0075). The digital twin platform supports the identification of potential issues and enhances overall plant performance [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0080).

2. The vertical fusion of IIoT and digital twin
-----------------------------------------------

The integration of DT into the IIoT framework is beneficial and necessary for the future of industrial automation. By bridging the gap between the physical and digital worlds, digital twins enable industries to achieve unprecedented efficiency and reliability. Here are some benefits of integrating DT with IIoT:
*   1.Performance optimization 
*   2.Downtime reduction 
*   3.Condition and probability maintenance 
*   4.Real-time monitoring [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0085) 
*   5.Anomaly and fault detection 
*   6.Aging and fault prediction 
*   7.Different real-time and parallel scenario analysis 
*   8.Cybersecurity and cyber vulnerability identification [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0090) 
*   9.Energy efficiency and cost reduction [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0095) 
*   10.Deep learning implementation 
*   11.Blockchain technology implementation [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0100) 

The IIoT is revolutionizing power generation connecting power units (generators, transformers, etc.) and processes through automation, data analytics, and distributed intelligent services [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0105). DT engineering has become a crucial methodology in future IoT and smart environments [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0110). Digital twins play a vital role in this transformation as a virtual replica of physical assets, industrial processes, and systems [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0115).

_A. The vertical IIoT concept_

IIoT devices and systems represent industrial facilities (industrial plants, large power systems, mines, factories, etc.) that generate large amounts of data [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0120). The fundamental characteristic of IIoT is the complete integration of operational and information technologies. Every smart power plant is essentially an integration of cyber-physical systems, IoT, internet services, computing at all levels (edge, fog, and cloud), wireless technology, automation, etc. The main three IIoT levels edge, fog, and cloud have their requirements, e.g., latency, privacy, or data transfer rate [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0125). The important decisions for the presented DT concept include positioning the hierarchical computing nodes [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0130). The vertical multi-level IIoT concept ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0005)) is defined according to the data-information-decision-making direction [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0135).

Fig. 1. Vertical Industrial IoT (IIoT) concept.

The vertical IoT concept in the industry, with clearly defined levels of edge, fog, and cloud computing, enables sophisticated data processing and optimization of industrial processes through hierarchical processing architecture. Each level plays a specific role in data collection, analysis, and utilization, allowing for more efficient decision-making and improved performance within the industrial ecosystem.

Edge computing is a paradigm for data processing and computing closest to the data sources (smart sensors, monitoring systems, and RTUs) minimizing latency and reducing the need for constant cloud connectivity. In IIoT, edge computing enables real-time data analysis and decision-making, which is critical for time-sensitive applications such as predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and process soptimization. With all the advantages of edge computing, two crucial facts are often overlooked: vulnerability to cyber-attacks and the impossibility of applying complex AI-supported decision-making algorithms.

Fog computing extends the capabilities of edge computing by providing additional processing power and storage closer to the edge but not on the device itself. It aggregates data from multiple edge devices, performs intermediate processing, and forwards relevant information to the cloud.

The cloud level is responsible for the complex data/information processing obtained from the fog (SCADA) level. At the highest IIoT hierarchical (cloud) level, complex decision-making algorithms should be based on complex ML models and ensembles. Software applications in the cloud are responsible for running AI models and decision-making algorithms.

The vertical IIoT concept is fully aligned with the in-depth security strategy. In-depth security, also known as defense in depth, is a strategy that employs multiple levels of security measures to protect an organization’s assets. If one level is breached, additional levels will remain in place to stop the threat. The highlighted grey areas ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0005)) between the levels contain numerous security tools such as firewalls, antivirus software, intrusion detection systems, etc.

AR and DT development strategies are the foundation for intuitively integrating human operators into the next generation of human cyber-physical systems [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0135) in the upcoming Industry 5.0 era. Today, ‘digital twins’ are a major research topic focusing on ‘smart cities’ [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0140) and ‘smart manufacturing’ [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0145), with high expectations for practical implementation in the Industry 5.0 era.

Digital twins are digital representations of physical systems or processes that serve as their digital “mirror images” created based on mathematical models for simulation, integration, testing, monitoring, and real-time maintenance [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0030). Vice versa, a Digital Twin is a dynamic, real-time, and holistic digital replica of a physical system (e.g., a power grid, a generator, or a wind farm) that is continuously updated with real-time data from sensors, IoT devices, and other sources.

It goes beyond simulation and analysis to include real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and decision-making. It is often part of a larger ecosystem that integrates AI, machine learning, and big data analytics. It can be implemented in cloud.

Using sensors and IoT technologies, data from the physical twin (production level devices) is transferred to the virtual twin, allowing engineers and managers to predict potential failures, optimize operating conditions, and improve production efficiency. The application of digital twins in the power system enables the simulation and testing of electricity generation and transmission scenarios, the integration of renewable energy sources, and the management of electricity consumption. The digital twin can continuously adapt and improve through ML models and algorithms. The state-of-the-art approach of the digital twin concepts offers the possibility of fitting into different modern optimization and management strategies [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0150).

_B. Digital twin concept_

In recent years, as an essential technology in the cyber-physical system, DT ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0010)) has been applied to various technological functions, phases, and cycles, including real-time simulation, remote monitoring, diagnostics, prediction, and control. Systems based on DT models and Augmented Reality (AR) are used to develop future systems based on human–machine interaction principles.

_C. The fusion concept_

Digital twins have emerged as a transformative technology in the energy sector. We are currently at an intersection between the two approaches regarding the electricity and industrial sectors. The first approach focuses on DT software tools for process and equipment control and monitoring. The second approach recognizes the need for a unified integration of DT and IIoT frameworks. This paper presents the vertical hierarchical framework of merged DT and IIoT concepts ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0015)), achieving a comprehensive control and monitoring approach.

The authors have identified the following shortcomings, which are presented in most research articles dealing with digital twins in an industrial environment:
*   1.Partial monitoring and modeling of systems or processes. 
*   2.Unclear relationships between the implemented models. 
*   3.Lack of unified management and decision-making strategy. 
*   4.Vulnerability to cyber-attacks and lack of in-depth security strategy. 

The proposed strategy largely overcomes the shortcomings mentioned. The main advantages of this approach are:
*   1.Hierarchical data processing and management in both DT spaces. 
*   2.Clearly defined functionalities and the merge zone between physical and virtual space (grey zone in [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0015)). 
*   3.Vertical in-depth security strategy with the ability to deploy independent cyber protection zones between the edge, fog, and cloud levels ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0005)). 

It is necessary to define a unique overlapping space for the physical and virtual parts of DT. Cloud computing should be functionally related to the output data and information obtained from the highest virtual twin level.

As the power industry embraces IIoT, deploying machine learning (ML) models at edge, fog, and cloud levels becomes crucial for achieving efficient and effective operations. The first two questions that need to be answered are which features and parameters should be selected and tuned to process optimization. The second step is determining feature correlation (correlation matrix) and tuning hyperparameters (accuracy, F1 score, etc.).

In the context of digital twins, the integration of ML models at edge and fog levels enhances the capabilities of these virtual replicas by providing real-time and aggregated insights:
*   1.Edge-level DT integration: 
*   •Real-time simulation: edge devices update digital twins with real-time data, enabling immediate simulation and feedback loops. 
*   •Low-level decision-making: edge ML models feed real-time data into digital twins, providing warnings and alerts enabling quick reactions to faults and anomalies. 
*   2.Fog-level DT integration: 
*   •Comprehensive analysis: fog computing aggregates data from multiple edge devices, providing a broader context for the digital twins to analyze and simulate. 
*   •Condition-based maintenance: fog-level ML models enhance in-depth analytics, helping to optimize operations and maintenance schedules. 

In the fusion concept ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0015)), cloud services and computing are responsible for complex decision-making. The presented fusion concept at the highest decision-making level relies on existing software cloud solutions and is not required to create unique DT applications. A fuzzy decision-making method should be used at the highest computational cloud level of the vertical DT concept.

3. Novel DT methodology and ML models
-------------------------------------

Nowadays, computing resources provide unprecedented opportunities for the hands-on digital twins' concept at the fog level. Machine learning models at the fog level are complex but fast enough for classification, regression, and image processing applied in quasi-real time for failure tracking, monitoring and prediction, aging prediction, and maintenance decision-making. Capital power units (generators and transformers) are managed, controlled, and monitored through many parameters (voltage, current, temperature, vibrations, flow, magnetic flux, etc.). Proper parameter selection, correlation functions (heat maps), measurement ranges and limits, output response speed to the input control signal, etc. are just some of the engineering tasks at the fog level. # Digital twins and generative AI: A powerful pairing

![](/~/media/mckinsey/our people/alex cosmas/alex cosmas-15140_fc-mask_headshot_988x741.png?cq=50&mw=128&car=1:1&cpy=Center)

![Sohrab Rahimi](/~/media/mckinsey/business functions/mckinsey digital/tech forward blog/profiles/bio-sohrab_rahimi-988x741.jpg?cq=50&mw=128&car=1:1&cpy=Center)

![Sanchit Tiwari](/~/media/mckinsey/business functions/mckinsey digital/tech forward blog/profiles/bio-sanchit_tiwari-988x741.jpg?cq=50&mw=128&car=1:1&cpy=Center)

April 11, 2024Many organizations across industries are separately implementing [digital twins](/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/what-is-digital-twin-technology) and [generative AI (gen AI)](/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/what-is-generative-ai)âtwo technologies with distinct value propositions and tremendous promiseâto support a wide range of use cases. Gen AI can structure inputs and synthesize outputs of digital twins, and digital twins can provide a robust test-and-learn environment for gen AI. By combining these technologies, organizations could produce synergies that reduce costs, accelerate deployment, and provide substantially more value than either could deliver on its own.

## About QuantumBlack, AI by McKinsey

This blog is the first in a three-part series on digital twins. Future posts will explore when and why to use digital twins and delve into an in-depth case study on how gen AI and digital twins could be used to enhance customer experience.

## Digital twins and gen AI have proven their value

Each of these technologies has demonstrated its value across a wide range of industries and use cases.

*Digital twins*âreusable virtual representations of assets, people, or processes and their environments that simulate strategies and optimize behaviorsâare a powerful tool that can help organizations improve data-backed decision making. By constructing scenarios of real-world situations and outcomes, they can provide insights that serve as an early-warning system, predicting events and the likelihood they will occur. They also provide a risk-free digital laboratory for testing designs and options, improving efficiency and time to market, for example, by optimizing scheduling, sequencing, and maintenance.

*Gen AI* describes algorithms that can be used to create content, including audio, code, images, text, simulations, and videos. As with digital twins, gen AI is revolutionizing business processes and activities in myriad ways, and new applications of gen AI are being conceived daily; the impact on productivity and economic potential multiplies with each one.

Today, [75 percent of large enterprises](/industries/industrials-and-electronics/our-insights/digital-twins-the-key-to-smart-product-development) are actively investing in digital twins to scale AI solutions. Each technology has immense potential, and together they could unlock trillions in total economic value.

## Generative AI and digital twins elevate and reinforce each other

Digital twins and gen AI can be used in combination to unlock insights and elevate their respective value. Following are some examples.

### A universal model

Building a digital twin, especially for highly specialized applications (such as multimachine production scheduling or vehicle routing), can be time-consuming and resource-intensive. The effort often entails designing and developing new digital-twin models, a process that can take six months or longer and incur substantial labor, computing, and server costs. By leveraging a software development platform such as GitHub, large language models (LLMs) can create code for the digital twin, accelerating the development process and increasing effectiveness. This ability to generate such output leads to an exciting prospect: LLMs could possibly be used to create a generalized digital-twin solutionâa foundational, universal modelâthat facilitates design and serves as a starting point for developers across digital-twin projects and even industries.

The architecture of a digital twin can be represented as nodes and edges in a time-series graph, allowing graph-based LLMs (such as GNN-LM, TextGNN, GIANT, and SimTeG) to create a basic model of a twin, whose design can then be built upon and adapted for various scenarios and industries.1 For example, in a future smart-city context, various urban elementsâsuch as traffic systems, public utilities, and environmental sensorsâcould feed into LLMs, which could identify and create potential connections between nodes in a graph. This lays the groundwork for simulating and predicting interactions and impacts over time.2

### Data collection, transfer, and augmentation

Digital twins thrive on large volumes of (often real-time) data from diverse sources, which can be unwieldly to manage. LLMs offer advanced âembeddingâ capabilities,3 meaning they can significantly compress data while retaining essential information. As such, they enable efficient data transfer and processing in digital twins.4 In a manufacturing setting, for example, gen AI could organize data from maintenance logs, equipment images, and operational videos. A digital twin could analyze this data, identify patterns or anomalies that might not be evident from unstructured data alone, and inform decision making and predictive-maintenance strategies.5

Additionally, gen AI tools can supplement data training sets used by digital twins by creating synthetic data. For example, a set of maintenance logs may not include a particular defect. Gen AI could create a synthetic data set that includes the defect so that the digital twin is trained to recognize it in the future.

### Interface

Digital twins generate a large volume of data on assets, people, or processes and their environments. Multimodal LLMs (such as Gemini and GPT-4V) can efficiently analyze and interpret large data sets and synthesize insights. Additionally, LLMs can generate scenarios for the digital twin to simulate that are more comprehensive. In this way, LLMs (such as OptiGuide or Vertex) can function as an interface for simulators, allowing users to communicate with digital twins via natural language (for example, asking questions and receiving understandable insights in return).6 This method eases interactions with a complex system, making it more accessible to users who lack extensive technical expertise, and facilitates data-backed decision making.

### Checks and balances

Gen AI not only enhances digital-twin capabilities but also uses real-time data from digital twins as a source of additional context, making inputs (such as prompt engineering) far more dynamic. With stores of robust, contextualized data, digital twins provide a secure environment in which gen AI can âlearnâ and broaden the scope of prompts and output. Through âwhat ifâ simulations run by digital twins, users can fine-tune gen AI, enabling it to conduct predictive modeling, as opposed to the primarily backward-looking view that most LLMs provide. Last, the digital-twin constraint engine can validate gen AI capabilities and boost gen AI accuracy by limiting answers to only feasible regions, helping answers from gen AI adhere to physical limits or other constraints. For example, after gen AI copilots generate code to support a piece of machinery, the digital twin can validate the code before it is deployed to ensure it functions within preset parameters (such as machinery temperature or output rate). While this capability is still in the early stages of development, there is immense potential and substantial future value for a broad variety of use cases.

## Capturing synergies requires mitigating certain risks

Leaders can mitigate risks associated with AI tools by establishing [clear principles](/capabilities/quantumblack/how-we-help-clients/generative-ai/responsible-ai-principles), a guiding framework and commitment to safe, secure use. Gen AI use comes with a potential for inaccuracies and bias, so it is important to ensure that tools are optimized for privacy and regulated data. Extensive diligence is necessary to assess the values and security measures of third parties prior to starting development. During the development process, it is important to carefully consider modelsâ assumptions and simplifications to ensure outputs are contextualized. Ongoing monitoring is also critical because inaccurate models are apt to produce misleading outcomes that the model itself is unlikely to flag. This can be avoided by ensuring that digital twins and gen AI are built on a foundation of high-volume and [high-quality data](/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/the-data-dividend-fueling-generative-ai). Prior to building a digital twin, it is important to have a clear business use case for the twin and safe, quality data available.

Generative AI can present ethical challenges, particularly with respect to data privacy and security. These risks are most prevalent in sensitive industries that handle personally identifiable information (PII). Addressing these challenges requires careful attention to data protection and ethically sourcing training data. Likewise, maintaining a culture of continuous learning and development of AI systems will be important as AI tools continue to evolve and advance. Regular adaptive training, improving feedback loops, and educating users are critical to success and maintaining compliance with legal and societal standards.

The symbiotic relationship between digital twins and gen AI increases their combined scalability, accessibility, and affordability. This new frontier will allow innovative and dynamic organizations to improve their advantage and allow organizations that are lagging behind to catch up to the competition.

--- SOURCE 4: What is Digital Twin Technology? ---
URL: https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/digital-twin/

SUMMARY:
A digital twin is a virtual model of a physical object. It spans the object's lifecycle and uses real-time data sent from sensors on the object to simulate the behavior and monitor operations. Digital twins can replicate many real-world items, from single pieces of equipment in a factory to full installations, such as wind turbines and even entire cities. Digital twin technology allows you to oversee the performance of an asset, identify potential faults, and make better-informed decisions about maintenance and lifecycle.

## [Exploring the Synergy Between Digital Twin Technology and Artificial Intelligence: A Comprehensive Survey](../../../Downloads/Volume16No3/Paper_99-Exploring_the_Synergy_Between_Digital_Twin_Technology.pdf "PDF")

Author 1: Wael Y. Alghamdi

Author 2: Rayan M. Alshamrani

Author 10: Atouf S Alazwari

International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications(IJACSA), Volume 16 Issue 3, 2025.

Abstract: The integration of Digital Twin Technology with Artificial Intelligence (AI) represents a transformative advancement across multiple domains. Digital twins are dynamic, real-time virtual representations of physical systems, leveraging technologies such as Internet of Things (IoT), augmented and virtual reality (AR/VR), big data analytics, 3D modeling, and cloud computing. Initially conceptualized by Michael Grieves in 2003 and further developed by organizations such as NASA, digital twins have been widely adopted in manufacturing, healthcare, smart cities, and energy systems. This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of how real-time data streams, continuous feedback loops, and predictive analytics within digital twins enhance AI capabilities, enabling anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, and data-driven decision-making. Additionally, the study examines technical and operational challenges, including data integration, sensor accuracy, cybersecurity, and computational overhead. By evaluating current methodologies and identifying future research directions, this survey underscores the potential of digital twins to drive adaptive, intelligent, and resilient systems in an increasingly data-driven world.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The convergence of AI and IoT enhances digital twins, enabling real-time data analysis, predictive maintenance, and improved operational efficiency through intelligent decision-making.
Digital twins contribute to sustainability by optimizing resource usage, reducing waste, and facilitating eco-friendly designs in infrastructure projects, aligning with global sustainability goals.
Collaborations between tech companies and infrastructure providers foster innovation in digital twin technology, driving the development of comprehensive, robust solutions for complex challenges.

Emphasizing scalability and interoperability ensures that digital twin systems can grow with organizational needs, integrating seamlessly across diverse platforms and technologies for enhanced functionality.
